_meta.authoringNotes: Classical Type III hypersensitivity per Lawley et al. NEJM 1984 and reviews by Hebert et al. JACI 1991. Modern drug- and monoclonal antibody-induced cases per Karmacharya et al. 2015 (PMID 26404577). Distinction of serum sickness-like reaction per Vial et al. 1995 and Heckbert 1990. Treatment per Bayer-Jaussi & Bircher Curr Opin Allergy Clin Immunol 2010.

h1Title: Serum Sickness: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Serum sickness is a type III hypersensitivity reaction caused by circulating immune complexes that deposit in skin, joints, kidneys, and other tissues, producing fever, urticaria, arthralgia, lymphadenopathy, and proteinuria roughly 7-21 days after exposure to a foreign protein or drug. Originally described after horse serum was used as antitoxin in diphtheria and tetanus, the classical form is now rare. Contemporary cases follow exposure to anti-thymocyte globulin in transplant, rituximab and other chimeric or humanized monoclonal antibodies (3-15% incidence depending on the agent), antivenom for snake or spider bite, and intravenous streptokinase. A clinically similar serum sickness-like reaction follows oral antibiotics — most often cefaclor — and other drugs in children, typically 7-14 days into the course. Self-limited disease usually resolves within 1-2 weeks with discontinuation of the offending agent, antihistamines, NSAIDs, and short-course oral corticosteroids; severe organ involvement is rare but warrants high-dose corticosteroids and occasional plasmapheresis.

keyStats.prevalence: Modern incidence under 1 per 10,000 exposures to foreign protein therapies overall; up to 30% for first-time horse antithymocyte globulin and 3-15% for rituximab in autoimmune disease
keyStats.demographics: Affects all ages; serum sickness-like reactions disproportionately reported in children under 6 receiving oral cefaclor
keyStats.avgAge: Adult presentations vary by drug exposure; pediatric cefaclor reactions peak ages 2-6 years
keyStats.globalCases: Several thousand reported cases per year worldwide; under-reported because of self-limited course and misattribution to viral illness

definition: Serum sickness (ICD-10: T80.6 anaphylactic reaction due to serum; T80.61 other serum reaction following blood and blood products; T80.69 other serum reaction) is a delayed, immune complex-mediated systemic reaction occurring 7-21 days after first exposure or 1-7 days after re-exposure to a foreign protein or hapten-protein. Antibodies (typically IgM and IgG) form against the foreign antigen and combine to produce circulating immune complexes that activate complement, deposit in small vessels of skin, joints, kidneys, and other tissues, and trigger neutrophil-mediated vasculitis. Coombs and Gell classified this as type III hypersensitivity. Classical serum sickness, named in 1905 by Clemens von Pirquet after his observations on horse-derived diphtheria antitoxin, includes fever, urticarial rash (often serpiginous), polyarthralgia, lymphadenopathy, glomerulonephritis, and decreased serum complement (C3, C4, CH50). A distinct entity called serum sickness-like reaction shares the clinical picture (fever, rash, arthralgia) but lacks immune complex deposition, hypocomplementemia, and renal involvement; it follows certain drugs including cefaclor, minocycline, bupropion, and infliximab. Differentiating serum sickness from infection, drug-induced lupus, Stevens-Johnson syndrome, DRESS, viral exanthem, and acute vasculitis is the central clinical task. Management is led by allergy and immunology with rheumatology, dermatology, and nephrology input when organ involvement is present.

typesClassification.description: Develops after exposure to foreign protein therapies (anti-thymocyte globulin, antivenom, streptokinase, monoclonal antibodies). Features immune complex deposition, hypocomplementemia, glomerulonephritis in severe cases, and proven circulating immune complexes. Onset 7-21 days after first exposure.

Clinical picture similar to classical serum sickness but lacks immune complex deposition, hypocomplementemia, and renal involvement. Most often follows oral antibiotics — cefaclor is the prototype — and other drugs. Onset 7-14 days into therapy; usually milder and resolves with drug discontinuation.

Increasingly recognized after rituximab, infliximab, omalizumab, abciximab, natalizumab, and other biologics. Mechanism is true immune complex formation against the foreign or chimeric portion of the antibody. Incidence varies by drug and indication; rituximab in autoimmune disease has 3-15% rates.

Common after equine-derived antivenom for snake, spider, scorpion, and jellyfish envenomation. Reported rates 20-75% in older equine antivenoms; modern Fab' fragments and ovine-derived antivenoms have substantially lower rates (under 10%).

typesClassification.type: Severe serum sickness with vasculitis and organ involvement
typesClassification.description: Rare manifestation with glomerulonephritis, peripheral neuropathy, myocarditis, or pericarditis. Requires high-dose corticosteroids, sometimes plasmapheresis, and prolonged immunosuppression.

primarySymptoms: Fever (38-40 °C), often spiking and accompanied by rigors, beginning 7-21 days after exposure or 1-7 days after re-exposure.
Urticarial or serpiginous rash, classically beginning at the site of injection (in injectable therapies) and spreading centrally, often along the lateral feet and hands, palms, and soles.
Symmetrical arthralgia and arthritis affecting hands, wrists, knees, and ankles, ranging from mild aching to disabling joint pain.
Generalized lymphadenopathy in 10-20%, particularly affecting cervical and axillary chains adjacent to the exposure site.
Generalized malaise, fatigue, headache, and myalgia.
Edema of the hands, feet, and face — sometimes with peri-orbital involvement.
Pruritus, ranging from mild itching to severe disturbance of sleep.
Abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting in 10-15% of cases, sometimes with mild hepatic enzyme elevation.
Proteinuria, hematuria, and red cell casts in classical serum sickness with glomerular involvement.
Peripheral neuropathy or transient cranial nerve palsies (especially brachial plexopathy) in severe cases.
earlyWarningSigns: New rash, joint pain, and low-grade fever 7-14 days after starting a new drug or receiving antivenom
Recurrent urticaria not responding to standard antihistamines, with associated fever
Unexplained polyarthralgia in a patient on biologic therapy (rituximab, infliximab, omalizumab)
Persistent fever after a course of oral cefaclor in a child
Tea-colored urine or peripheral edema after a recent foreign protein exposure
emergencySigns: Acute respiratory distress, stridor, or angioedema following drug exposure — exclude anaphylaxis
Rapidly rising creatinine, oliguria, or visible hematuria suggesting glomerulonephritis
New peripheral neuropathy or focal neurological deficit suggesting immune complex vasculitis
Chest pain, dyspnea, or arrhythmia indicating myocarditis or pericarditis
Severe purpuric or blistering rash with mucosal involvement — exclude Stevens-Johnson syndrome or DRESS

causes.description: Equine-derived anti-thymocyte globulin, snake and spider antivenom, antitoxin for diphtheria, tetanus, botulism, and rabies. These products contain large amounts of foreign protein; rates of serum sickness can reach 30% with classical preparations, falling to under 10% with modern Fab' fragments.

causes.cause: Chimeric, humanized, and human monoclonal antibodies
causes.description: Rituximab, infliximab, omalizumab, abciximab, natalizumab, and others. Reaction follows formation of antibodies against murine, chimeric, or even human regions of the therapeutic. Cumulative exposure increases risk, particularly with chimeric agents containing 25% murine protein.

causes.cause: Oral antibiotics — especially cefaclor in children
causes.description: Cefaclor is the classic cause of serum sickness-like reaction in children, with an incidence of 0.024-0.2% per course. Other implicated antibiotics include penicillins, cephalosporins (cefprozil, cefuroxime), trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, and minocycline. Mechanism is reactive metabolite-protein adduct rather than circulating immune complex.

causes.cause: Streptokinase, urokinase, and other thrombolytic and fibrinolytic agents
causes.description: Streptokinase, derived from streptococci, has approximately a 2-3% reported rate of serum sickness when given for myocardial infarction or stroke. Reaction can complicate retreatment within 5 years and limit re-exposure.

Rare cases follow rabies vaccine, hepatitis B vaccine, anti-Rh immunoglobulin, and intravenous immunoglobulin. Most reactions are mild and self-limited. Serum sickness has been described after a number of monoclonal antibody therapies for cancer, autoimmunity, and infectious disease.
Less commonly, small-molecule drugs trigger a serum sickness-like reaction through hapten-protein binding. The clinical phenotype is identical to other SSLR; resolution follows drug discontinuation.

riskFactors.factor: Prior exposure to the same foreign protein
riskFactors.description: Re-exposure produces a faster onset (1-7 days) and more severe reaction. Patients who received horse antitoxin, equine antivenom, or initial dose of rituximab carry sustained immunologic memory and higher risk on rechallenge.

riskFactors.factor: Treatment with chimeric or humanized monoclonal antibodies
riskFactors.description: Chimeric agents (containing 25% murine protein) such as rituximab and infliximab have higher rates than fully human antibodies. Risk also rises with cumulative dose and longer treatment courses.
Equine-derived antivenom for envenomation in tropical and subtropical regions, and anti-thymocyte globulin in solid organ and stem cell transplantation, carry the highest contemporary rates.
Children aged 2-6 receiving cefaclor have the highest reported rates of SSLR, possibly because of unique drug metabolism producing reactive intermediates. Adults can also be affected.

Patients with lupus, vasculitis, or rheumatoid arthritis treated with rituximab or other biologics may have a higher reported rate of serum sickness, possibly because of pre-existing immune activation and complement consumption.

riskFactors.factor: High cumulative dose of monoclonal antibody
riskFactors.description: Risk is dose- and duration-dependent for many monoclonal antibodies. Pre-medication and gradual escalation reduce but do not eliminate risk.

Pre-existing atopic disease and a history of drug allergy modestly raise the likelihood of serum sickness and serum sickness-like reactions, although true serum sickness can affect non-atopic patients.

affectedDemographics: Modern overall incidence under 1 per 10,000 exposures to foreign proteins
Anti-thymocyte globulin: 10-30% in first courses; 50-80% in repeat exposures historically
Rituximab: 3-15% in autoimmune disease cohorts; far lower in lymphoma
Cefaclor in children: 0.024-0.2% per prescription; lower with cefuroxime and other cephalosporins
Antivenom: under 10% with modern Fab' fragments; up to 75% with older equine preparations in some regions
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis rests on the characteristic clinical pattern of fever, urticarial rash, arthralgia, and lymphadenopathy appearing 7-21 days after exposure to a foreign protein or drug. History identifies the specific exposure, prior similar reactions, and any concurrent medications. Physical examination documents the rash distribution (especially serpiginous lesions on the lateral feet and hands), joint involvement, lymph nodes, and edema. Routine investigations include complete blood count (often shows neutrophilia, eosinophilia, and mild anemia), urinalysis (proteinuria, hematuria, red cell casts in classical disease), creatinine and electrolytes (renal involvement), liver function tests, ESR and CRP (elevated), and serum complement (C3, C4, CH50 reduced in classical serum sickness but normal in SSLR). Complement consumption is the single most useful test to distinguish true serum sickness (low) from serum sickness-like reaction (normal). Anti-drug antibody testing can confirm immune response to specific monoclonal antibodies. Skin biopsy of fresh lesions shows leukocytoclastic vasculitis or perivascular neutrophilic infiltrate with immune complex deposits on direct immunofluorescence. Renal biopsy is reserved for unclear cases with severe glomerulonephritis. The differential diagnosis includes drug-induced lupus, hypersensitivity vasculitis, Stevens-Johnson syndrome, DRESS, acute viral exanthem (parvovirus, hepatitis B, EBV, hepatitis C), Henoch-Schonlein purpura, Kawasaki disease (in children), and acute rheumatic fever. Drug allergy testing is generally not performed during the acute reaction but can confirm sensitization 4-6 weeks later if rechallenge is considered.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the offending agent and the temporal relationship (7-21 days after first exposure, 1-7 days after re-exposure)
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Structured clinical interview reviewing all drug, biologic, antivenom, and blood product exposures over the prior 4 weeks. Often the single most informative diagnostic step.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes classical serum sickness (consumed complement) from serum sickness-like reaction (normal complement)
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw, results return within 24-48 hours. C3 and C4 typically fall below the laboratory reference range during active disease.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies glomerular involvement (proteinuria, hematuria, red cell casts) in classical disease
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Urine collected in clinic and analyzed by dipstick and microscopy. Repeat samples monitor evolution. Serum creatinine returns same day.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Document inflammatory response and rule out other diagnoses
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. Neutrophilia and eosinophilia are common; ESR and CRP are typically elevated.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms leukocytoclastic vasculitis with immune complex deposits in difficult cases
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Outpatient punch biopsy of a fresh lesion under local anesthesia. Histology and immunofluorescence reports within 1-2 weeks.

diagnosticTests.test: Anti-drug antibody testing for monoclonal antibodies
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms immune response to the specific therapeutic and informs future treatment decisions
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Specialized blood test through immunology laboratories; results in 1-4 weeks.

treatmentOverview: Management has four pillars: prompt discontinuation of the offending agent, symptomatic relief, treatment of organ involvement when present, and prevention of recurrence. The single most important step is identifying and stopping the responsible drug or biologic, after which most cases resolve within 1-2 weeks. Symptomatic treatment includes second-generation antihistamines (cetirizine 10 mg, fexofenadine 180 mg, loratadine 10 mg daily) for urticaria and pruritus, paracetamol or short-course NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400-600 mg three times daily, naproxen 500 mg twice daily) for fever and arthralgia, and short-course oral corticosteroids (prednisolone 0.5-1 mg/kg/day for 5-10 days with rapid taper) for moderate or persistent disease. Severe systemic disease — glomerulonephritis, peripheral neuropathy, myocarditis — requires high-dose intravenous methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 3-5 days, sometimes with plasmapheresis or intravenous immunoglobulin. After resolution, the offending drug should be entered into the allergy record and avoided in future. Re-exposure to the same monoclonal antibody is sometimes attempted with desensitization and pre-medication when no alternative therapy exists. For antivenom-related serum sickness, future envenomation should be treated with Fab' fragments or alternative species-derived products where possible. Cefaclor-induced serum sickness-like reactions in children rarely cross-react with other beta-lactams and a structured allergy workup may safely allow penicillin and amoxicillin use after recovery. Long-term follow-up includes monitoring of complement, urinalysis, and renal function in classical cases, and review of the allergy action plan at every healthcare encounter.

medicalTreatments.name: Discontinuation of the offending drug or biologic
medicalTreatments.description: The cornerstone of treatment. Symptoms typically begin to improve within 24-72 hours and resolve over 1-2 weeks. Reintroduction is avoided unless no alternative exists and desensitization is planned.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Resolution of symptoms in over 90% of mild to moderate cases within 14 days

medicalTreatments.name: Second-generation oral antihistamines (cetirizine 10 mg, fexofenadine 180 mg, loratadine 10 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line for urticaria, pruritus, and mild systemic features. Continued for the duration of symptoms; higher than label doses are sometimes used for refractory urticaria.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom reduction in 60-80% of cutaneous and mild systemic disease

medicalTreatments.name: NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400-600 mg three times daily, naproxen 500 mg twice daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Adjunctive for fever, arthralgia, and mild myalgia. Limited to short courses to minimize gastric and renal risk, especially in patients with active renal involvement.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptomatic relief of fever and arthralgia in 60-75% of patients

medicalTreatments.name: Oral corticosteroids (prednisolone 0.5-1 mg/kg/day for 5-10 days with rapid taper)
medicalTreatments.description: Used for moderate symptoms (high fever, marked arthritis, extensive rash, lymphadenopathy) and refractory cases. Tapered over 7-14 days.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Faster symptom resolution in 80-90% of moderate cases; reduces duration by 3-5 days versus supportive care alone

medicalTreatments.name: High-dose intravenous methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 3-5 days
medicalTreatments.description: Reserved for severe disease with organ involvement: glomerulonephritis, peripheral neuropathy, myocarditis, or vasculitis with progressive features. Followed by oral taper.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom and laboratory improvement in 70-85% of severe cases within 1-2 weeks

medicalTreatments.name: Plasmapheresis or intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG 2 g/kg)
medicalTreatments.description: Considered in steroid-refractory severe disease or progressive organ failure. Plasmapheresis removes circulating immune complexes; IVIG modulates immune response.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Case series suggest benefit in 60-80% of refractory cases; no randomized trials due to rarity of disease

alternativeTreatments.name: Pre-medication and desensitization for essential monoclonal antibody therapy
alternativeTreatments.description: Premedication with antihistamines, corticosteroids, and slower infusion rates may reduce reaction severity. Desensitization protocols enable continued therapy when no alternative exists.

alternativeTreatments.name: Substitution with humanized or fully human alternatives
alternativeTreatments.description: For chimeric monoclonal antibodies (rituximab, infliximab), substituting with humanized or fully human alternatives (ocrelizumab, golimumab, adalimumab) reduces immunogenicity in subsequent treatment courses.

whySeeSpecialist: Any patient with suspected serum sickness should be evaluated by allergy and immunology, both to confirm the diagnosis and to plan safe future therapy. Nephrology, neurology, or cardiology input is warranted when organ involvement is present. Rheumatology assessment helps distinguish serum sickness from drug-induced lupus or systemic vasculitis.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose an allergy specialist with experience in drug allergy, biologics, and antivenom-related reactions. Tertiary centers with multidisciplinary biologics clinics offer the best combination of immunology, rheumatology, and nephrology expertise for severe or rare presentations.
hospitalCriteria: Allergy and immunology service with drug allergy expertise
Rheumatology, nephrology, and dermatology consultation availability
keyFacilities: Solid organ and stem cell transplantation centers
preventionStrategies: Document and communicate previous serum sickness reactions in the medical record and on a personal allergy card.
Use the lowest effective dose and shortest course of monoclonal antibody therapy where clinically appropriate.
Prefer humanized or fully human monoclonal antibodies over chimeric agents when alternatives exist.
Use Fab' fragments or other species-derived antivenoms instead of older equine preparations where available.
Pre-medicate before high-risk infusions (antihistamine plus corticosteroid as per institutional protocol).
Avoid cefaclor in children with a history of serum sickness-like reaction to it; consider alternative antibiotics.
lifestyleModifications: Rest and hydrate during the acute phase; symptoms typically resolve over 1-2 weeks.
Avoid the offending agent for life unless desensitization has been planned by a specialist.
Carry a medical alert card or bracelet listing the responsible drug.
Notify every prescriber of the prior reaction at every visit, including pharmacies.
Continue all medications prescribed for the underlying condition (e.g., disease-modifying therapy) under specialist guidance.
Attend allergy follow-up at 4-6 weeks to confirm resolution and discuss future safe therapies.

recommended: Balanced diet with adequate hydration during the acute phase
Foods rich in protein, vitamins, and minerals to support recovery
Avoidance of new dietary triggers during illness to simplify symptom interpretation
Anti-inflammatory diet (Mediterranean style) during recovery in patients with concurrent rheumatologic disease
avoid: Alcohol while on corticosteroids or NSAIDs
Excessive caffeine or stimulants during fever and tachycardia
Foods with known cross-reactivity if a specific allergen is implicated
Grapefruit juice in patients on calcineurin inhibitors or steroids with relevant interactions

exerciseGuidelines: Rest during the acute phase. Gentle activity (walking, light stretching) can resume once fever and joint pain settle. Return to vigorous exercise after symptoms have fully resolved, usually 2-4 weeks. Avoid contact sports during corticosteroid taper if myocarditis was suspected.
dailyManagement: Take prescribed antihistamines, NSAIDs, and corticosteroids at the same time each day.
Monitor temperature, rash distribution, joint symptoms, and urine color daily during the acute phase.
Report new or worsening symptoms (chest pain, oliguria, neurological symptoms) immediately.
Drink at least 2 L of fluid daily unless restricted.
Keep a symptom and medication diary to share with the allergist at follow-up.
Attend laboratory monitoring (complement, urinalysis, creatinine) as scheduled until resolution.
prognosis: Most patients recover completely within 1-2 weeks of stopping the offending agent and beginning supportive care; mortality is under 0.1% in modern series, and major organ damage is rare. Cefaclor-induced serum sickness-like reactions in children have an excellent prognosis with full recovery in over 95% of cases. Severe classical serum sickness with glomerulonephritis or vasculitis has a more variable course but rarely produces lasting renal damage when treated with high-dose corticosteroids. Recurrence with re-exposure to the same antigen is common and typically more rapid in onset. Long-term immunologic consequences are limited; patients retain memory B-cells against the offending antigen but do not have ongoing autoimmune disease. Avoidance of the responsible agent prevents future episodes in nearly all cases.
recoveryTimeline: Symptom resolution typically begins within 24-72 hours of stopping the offending agent. Rash and arthralgia improve over 7-14 days; lymphadenopathy and laboratory abnormalities (low complement, proteinuria) resolve over 2-6 weeks. Severe cases with renal or neurological involvement may take 2-3 months to fully recover.
complications: Glomerulonephritis with proteinuria, hematuria, and reduced GFR — typically self-limited but warrants nephrology follow-up and may require corticosteroid therapy.
Peripheral neuropathy (especially brachial plexus involvement) — usually resolves over weeks to months with corticosteroid therapy.
Myocarditis and pericarditis — rare but potentially life-threatening; requires intensive corticosteroid therapy.
Persistent urticaria and chronic spontaneous urticaria in occasional patients after acute resolution.
Anaphylaxis on re-exposure to the same agent — risk in any future encounter.
Cross-reactivity with related drugs (penicillins, cephalosporins) — important consideration for future antibiotic prescribing in cefaclor-related cases.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Allergy, Asthma and Immunology (AAAAI)
supportResources.description: Patient information on drug allergy and serum sickness from the leading US allergy society.

supportResources.name: European Academy of Allergy and Clinical Immunology (EAACI)
supportResources.description: European resources on drug hypersensitivity reactions including serum sickness and SSLR.

supportResources.name: British Society for Allergy and Clinical Immunology (BSACI)
supportResources.description: UK allergy society with guidelines and patient information on drug allergy and serum sickness.

supportResources.name: MedlinePlus — Serum Sickness
supportResources.description: US National Library of Medicine patient information on serum sickness and serum sickness-like reactions.

confusedWithConditions.name: Anaphylaxis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Anaphylaxis is an IgE-mediated immediate hypersensitivity that occurs within minutes of exposure with hypotension, bronchospasm, and angioedema. Serum sickness is a delayed reaction (7-21 days) characterized by fever, urticaria, and arthralgia without acute hypotension. Treatment for anaphylaxis is epinephrine; serum sickness responds to corticosteroids and antihistamines.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lupus
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Drug-induced lupus shares some features with serum sickness (fever, arthralgia, rash) but typically presents with positive anti-histone antibodies, malar rash, and serositis without complement consumption. Serum sickness resolves rapidly with drug withdrawal while drug-induced lupus may persist longer; serology distinguishes the two.

confusedWithConditions.name: Food Allergy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Food allergy is typically IgE-mediated with immediate symptoms after ingestion. Serum sickness is a delayed immune complex reaction following parenteral or oral exposure to specific foreign proteins or drugs over 7-21 days. The mechanism and time course are distinct.

confusedWithConditions.name: Allergic Contact Dermatitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Contact dermatitis is a localized skin reaction at the site of allergen contact appearing 24-72 hours later as eczema. Serum sickness is a systemic reaction with widespread rash, fever, and joint involvement appearing 7-21 days after exposure. Differential testing (patch test vs. drug history) distinguishes them.

relatedConditions.name: Anaphylaxis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another drug-induced hypersensitivity reaction with different mechanism (IgE-mediated, immediate) but shared importance of identifying and avoiding the trigger.

relatedConditions.name: Lupus
relatedConditions.relevance: Drug-induced lupus shares clinical features with serum sickness; both are managed by avoidance of the offending agent and immunosuppression when severe.

relatedConditions.name: Food Allergy
relatedConditions.relevance: Both involve immune-mediated reactions to foreign proteins, although mechanisms differ. Patients with multiple allergies may need comprehensive evaluation.

relatedConditions.name: Allergic Contact Dermatitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another type of hypersensitivity reaction sharing the role of allergen identification and avoidance.

faqs.question: What is serum sickness in simple terms?
faqs.answer: Serum sickness is a delayed allergic reaction to medicines or animal-derived proteins. About 1-3 weeks after first exposure (or sooner after repeat exposure), patients develop fever, hives, joint pain, and swollen lymph nodes. Symptoms usually resolve within 1-2 weeks of stopping the trigger.

faqs.question: What causes serum sickness?
faqs.answer: Causes include monoclonal antibody drugs (rituximab, infliximab), animal-derived antivenom and antitoxin, anti-thymocyte globulin used in transplantation, streptokinase, and certain antibiotics (especially cefaclor in children). The reaction is due to immune complexes formed against the foreign protein.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of serum sickness?
faqs.answer: Classic symptoms are fever, urticarial rash often starting at the injection site, joint pain, swollen lymph nodes, fatigue, and edema. Severe cases may include kidney involvement (proteinuria), peripheral neuropathy, and rarely myocarditis. Symptoms typically appear 7-21 days after exposure.

faqs.question: How is serum sickness diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is based on clinical pattern and exposure history. Low complement levels (C3, C4) support classical serum sickness; normal complement suggests serum sickness-like reaction. Urinalysis, kidney function, and inflammatory markers complete the workup. Skin biopsy may be needed in unclear cases.

faqs.question: How is serum sickness treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment is discontinuing the offending agent and providing symptomatic relief with antihistamines, NSAIDs, and short-course oral corticosteroids in moderate cases. Severe disease with organ involvement requires high-dose intravenous methylprednisolone and occasionally plasmapheresis or IVIG.

faqs.question: How long does serum sickness last?
faqs.answer: Most cases resolve within 1-2 weeks of stopping the offending agent. Severe cases with kidney or neurological involvement may take 2-3 months for complete recovery. Recurrence is common with re-exposure to the same agent, and reactions are usually faster and more severe on rechallenge.

faqs.question: Is serum sickness dangerous?
faqs.answer: Most cases are self-limited and resolve completely with treatment. Severe disease with glomerulonephritis, peripheral neuropathy, or myocarditis is rare but potentially life-threatening. Mortality is under 0.1% in modern series. Early diagnosis and discontinuation of the agent prevent progression.

faqs.question: What is the difference between serum sickness and serum sickness-like reaction?
faqs.answer: Classical serum sickness involves true immune complex deposition, low complement levels, and possible kidney involvement. Serum sickness-like reaction has similar clinical symptoms (fever, rash, arthralgia) but normal complement and no immune complex deposits. SSLR most commonly follows cefaclor in children.

faqs.question: Can serum sickness happen after rituximab?
faqs.answer: Yes. Rituximab-induced serum sickness occurs in 3-15% of patients with autoimmune diseases, less commonly in lymphoma patients. Onset is typically 7-14 days after infusion. Switching to humanized alternatives like ocrelizumab may be considered for future therapy.

faqs.question: Can children get serum sickness?
faqs.answer: Yes. Serum sickness-like reactions in children are most commonly associated with oral cefaclor (rates of 0.024-0.2% per prescription). Children aged 2-6 are most affected. Classical serum sickness from monoclonal antibodies and antivenom can also occur in pediatric patients.

faqs.question: How long after exposure does serum sickness develop?
faqs.answer: First exposure produces symptoms after 7-21 days. Re-exposure to the same agent produces faster onset (1-7 days) and often more severe symptoms because of immunologic memory from the initial reaction.

faqs.question: What is the rash like in serum sickness?
faqs.answer: The rash is typically urticarial (hives) with a characteristic serpiginous pattern. It often begins at the injection site or on the lateral feet and hands, spreading centrally. The palms and soles are commonly involved. Pruritus is significant in most cases.

faqs.question: Can serum sickness affect the kidneys?
faqs.answer: Yes. Classical serum sickness can produce glomerulonephritis with proteinuria, hematuria, and red cell casts in about 10-20% of cases. Severe renal involvement is rare. Monitoring with urinalysis and serum creatinine is part of standard care during the acute phase.

faqs.question: Is serum sickness the same as anaphylaxis?
faqs.answer: No. Anaphylaxis is an IgE-mediated immediate hypersensitivity that occurs within minutes with hypotension, bronchospasm, and angioedema. Serum sickness is a delayed type III hypersensitivity occurring 7-21 days after exposure with fever, rash, and arthralgia. Treatments and mechanisms differ.

faqs.question: Can serum sickness be prevented?
faqs.answer: Avoid re-exposure to the agent that caused a previous reaction. Use humanized or human monoclonal antibodies rather than chimeric agents when possible. Pre-medicate before high-risk infusions with antihistamines and corticosteroids. Consider Fab' antivenom over equine preparations.

faqs.question: Will I need future allergy testing?
faqs.answer: Allergy testing is generally deferred until acute symptoms resolve, typically 4-6 weeks. Skin and serum tests for specific drugs help confirm the trigger and guide future treatment decisions. The offending agent is added to the patient's permanent allergy record.

faqs.question: Can I take penicillin if I had serum sickness from cefaclor?
faqs.answer: Cefaclor-induced serum sickness-like reaction in children rarely cross-reacts with other beta-lactams. A structured allergy workup typically allows safe use of penicillin and amoxicillin after recovery. Discuss with an allergy specialist before re-exposure to related antibiotics.

faqs.question: Do steroids help serum sickness?
faqs.answer: Yes. Short courses of oral prednisolone (0.5-1 mg/kg/day for 5-10 days with rapid taper) speed symptom resolution in moderate cases. High-dose intravenous methylprednisolone is reserved for severe disease with organ involvement and shows benefit in 70-85% of severe cases.

faqs.question: What is the rate of serum sickness after antivenom?
faqs.answer: Older equine-derived antivenoms have rates of 20-75% serum sickness in some populations. Modern Fab' fragment and ovine-derived antivenoms have substantially lower rates, generally under 10%. Pre-medication and slower infusion further reduce risk.

faqs.question: Can serum sickness recur?
faqs.answer: Yes. Re-exposure to the same agent produces faster onset (1-7 days) and often more severe symptoms because of immunologic memory. Avoidance of the responsible agent is the most effective prevention. In rare cases, desensitization protocols allow continued essential therapy.

faqs.question: Is serum sickness common after vaccines?
faqs.answer: Vaccine-associated serum sickness is rare. Reports describe occasional cases after rabies vaccine, hepatitis B vaccine, and certain monoclonal antibody therapies. Most cases are mild and self-limited. Vaccine benefit far outweighs the risk in nearly all patients.

symptomKeywords: rash and fever after new drug
metaTitle: Serum Sickness: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Serum sickness: delayed immune reaction to drugs and biologics with fever, hives, joint pain. Diagnosis, steroids and prevention, medically reviewed.
